message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://github.com/orgs/espeak-ng/discussions/2338
**Title:** The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users (Samuel Proulx / eSpeak-NG discussion, Jan 2026)
**Found via:** formant-synth-vs-neural/blind-power-users search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: Extract all claims related to: (1) why blind screen-reader power users prefer formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over neural TTS at high speed, (2) intelligibility vs naturalness trade-offs at high speaking rates (18-22 syllables/sec), (3) consistency, crispness, latency, predictability of formant vs neural synthesis, (4) any specific engines/voices mentioned and their speed/comprehensibility properties, (5) any quantitative figures (words per minute, syllables per second, percentages). Quote the exact text for each claim. Also note the publish date, author, and whether this is a forum discussion or authoritative source.

content.content:
# Analysis: Web Page Content

**Source Type:** GitHub Discussion (forum format, not authoritative article)

**Actual Content Available:** The webpage provided contains only a **discussion header and metadata**, not the actual article content.

**What's Present:**
- Discussion title: "The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users"
- Author: fabiolimace
- Date: January 14, 2026
- Link reference to external article: https://stuff.interfree.ca/2026/01/05/ai-tts-for-screenreaders.html (by Samuel Proulx, published January 5, 2026)
- Brief descriptor: "The past year has seen an explosion in new text to speech engines based on neural networks, large language models, and machine learning. But has any of this advancement offered anything to those using screen readers?"

**What's Missing:**
No specific claims about formant vs. neural TTS trade-offs, intelligibility metrics, latency comparisons, engine-specific performance data, or quantitative figures (WPM/syllables per second) are present in this webpage extract.

**Conclusion:**
The actual article content you're seeking exists at the external URL cited but is not included in the provided webpage HTML. To extract the claims you've requested, the full text of the referenced Proulx article would be needed.

content.text: The GitHub discussion just links to the original article. Let me fetch the actual source article by Samuel Proulx.

input.prompt: Extract all claims related to: (1) why blind screen-reader power users prefer formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over neural/AI TTS, especially at high speed, (2) intelligibility vs naturalness trade-offs at high speaking rates, (3) consistency, crispness, latency, predictability/responsiveness of formant vs neural synthesis, (4) any specific engines/voices mentioned and their speed/comprehensibility/responsiveness properties, (5) any quantitative figures (words per minute, syllables per second, percentages, latency in ms). Quote the exact text verbatim for each claim. Also note the publish date and author.

content.content:
# Claims Analysis: AI vs Formant TTS for Screen Reader Users

**Article:** "The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users"  
**Author:** Samuel Proulx  
**Published:** January 5, 2026

---

## (1) Why Blind Users Prefer Formant Synthesizers

**Claim on user preferences:**
> "blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient"

**Claim on voice characteristics:**
> "a preference among blind users for voices that sound somewhat robotic, but can be understood at high rates of speed"

---

## (2) Intelligibility vs Naturalness Trade-offs

**Claim on design conflict:**
> "The needs of a blind text to speech user are vastly different than those of a sighted user."

**Claim on AI design priorities:**
> "These modern systems are developed to sound human, natural, and conversational. Unfortunately this seems to come at the expense of accuracy."

---

## (3) Consistency, Crispness, Latency, Predictability

**Claim on parameter control loss:**
> "Older text to speech systems make changing the pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, headsize, and other parameters of the voice easy."

**Claim on neural TTS latency:**
> "Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text."

**Claim on control limitations:**
> "AI text to speech models, being trained on data from a particular set of speakers, cannot offer this customization."

---

## (4) Specific Engines and Properties

**Eloquence (formant):**
> "last updated in 2003" and described as overwhelmingly popular among Western English blind users

**eSpeak-ng:**
> "based directly on Speak, a text to speech system written by Jonathan Duddington in 1995 for RISC OS"

**Supertonic and Kitten TTS (neural):**
> "can stream result audio as it becomes available" (Supertonic) vs Kitten TTS "cannot start speaking until all of the audio for the chunk is fully generated"

---

## (5) Quantitative Figures

**Speaking rate preference:**
> "often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute"

**Average speaking rate (comparison baseline):**
> "The speaking rate of an average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute"

**Kitten TTS dependency count:**
> "around 103" dependencies; Supertonic "just over 30"

content.text: I have substantial content from the source article. Let me try to get a bit more detail on the latency and responsiveness claims to ensure I have accurate quotes.

input.prompt: Quote verbatim any sentences about: latency and responsiveness when navigating quickly with a screen reader; why streaming/responsiveness matters for screen reader users; how fast blind users listen and at what words-per-minute; the difference between formant synthesis responsiveness and neural TTS responsiveness; and any statement comparing the comprehensibility or intelligibility of robotic voices vs natural voices at high speed. Give exact quotes only.

content.content:
# Direct Quotes on Screen Reader TTS Responsiveness

**On listening speed:**
"often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute" and "The speaking rate of an average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute"

**On why responsiveness matters:**
"Users of screen readers quickly jump through text and frequently interrupt the screen reader, and thus require the text to speech system to be able to quickly discard and restart speech."

**On the responsiveness problem with modern AI TTS:**
"a text to speech system needs to begin generating speech as quickly as possible, rather than waiting for an entire phrase or sentence"

**On the technical limitation:**
"Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text."

**On voice preference trade-offs:**
"blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient. This results in a preference among blind users for voices that sound somewhat robotic, but can be understood at high rates of speed"

content.text: I now have comprehensive content. This is a primary source from the blind/screen-reader power-user community (Samuel Proulx, a known accessibility expert), surfaced via the official eSpeak-NG GitHub discussion. It directly addresses angle (2) and (5) of the research question. Let me compile the structured output.

claims.claim: Blind screen-reader power users prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient, which results in a preference for somewhat robotic-sounding voices that remain understandable at very high speeds, directly trading naturalness for intelligibility.
claims.quote: blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient. This results in a preference among blind users for voices that sound somewhat robotic, but can be understood at high rates of speed

claims.claim: Screen-reader power users commonly listen at 800-900 words per minute, roughly 3-4x the average human speaking rate of 200-250 wpm, establishing the extreme-speed regime where formant synthesizers are favored.
claims.quote: often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute

claims.claim: Modern neural/AI TTS systems are designed to sound human, natural, and conversational, but this naturalness comes at the expense of accuracy/intelligibility -- a core argument for why power listeners reject them.
claims.quote: These modern systems are developed to sound human, natural, and conversational. Unfortunately this seems to come at the expense of accuracy.

claims.claim: Latency/responsiveness is a decisive factor: screen reader users rapidly skip and interrupt speech, so the engine must begin producing audio immediately and restart instantly, whereas neural engines like Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot start until a full text chunk is available.
claims.quote: Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text.

claims.claim: Older formant synthesizers expose granular, real-time control over pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, and head size, while neural TTS trained on fixed speakers cannot offer this parameter customization -- limiting tunability for speed optimization.
claims.quote: AI text to speech models, being trained on data from a particular set of speakers, cannot offer this customization.

content.text:
I extracted the source. Note: the GitHub discussion URL (https://github.com/orgs/espeak-ng/discussions/2338) is just a pointer that links to the original article by Samuel Proulx at https://stuff.interfree.ca/2026/01/05/ai-tts-for-screenreaders.html (published 2026-01-05), which is where the actual content lives. I fetched and extracted from that primary article.

Source quality: primary — first-hand practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community, authored by a recognized accessibility expert and surfaced via the official eSpeak-NG project discussion. It directly addresses research angles (2) formant-vs-neural at extreme speed and (5)/(6) the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off, plus the under-appreciated latency/responsiveness dimension (instant interrupt/restart) that matters for local pipelines.

Five falsifiable claims returned, three rated central (the robotic-but-intelligible preference, the 800-900 wpm listening regime vs 200-250 wpm baseline, and the naturalness-comes-at-the-expense-of-accuracy thesis), with latency/responsiveness and parameter-controllability as supporting evidence.
